answer.text: The change in option premium for a one-day decrease in time to expiration (time decay)
The change in delta for a change in underlying price
The change in premium for a change in volatility
The change in premium for a change in interest rates

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Theta measures time decay — how much the option premium loses per day. Long option positions have NEGATIVE theta (lose value daily). Short positions have POSITIVE theta (benefit from time decay). **The Trap:** Theta is always present — options lose time value every day, even if the underlying doesn't move. **Cross-Exam Context:** Theta and time decay are tested in Series I and VIII. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Option sellers benefit from theta — this is why selling options is sometimes called 'selling time'. Time works against the buyer.
